mnemonics: Cantor's Diagonal Argument: Imagine a list of ALL real numbers. Cantor constructs a new number by changing the nth digit of the nth number. This new number can't be on the list, proving it's incomplete. The mnemonic is 'DIAGONAL DODGE' - you dodge every number on the list diagonally to create a new one.
keyPoints: Cantor's Theorem states that for any set X, the cardinality of its power set P(X) is strictly greater than the cardinality of X itself.
The proof uses a technique called 'diagonalization' to show that no function from a set to its power set can be surjective.
This theorem implies there is an infinite hierarchy of infinities, with no 'largest' infinite set.
commonMistakes: Confusing Cantor's Theorem (about power sets) with the Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein Theorem (about bijections between sets).
Misapplying the diagonal argument to finite sets or incorrectly assuming the argument is invalid because the new number is 'constructed'.
